hook: You already know how to list ordered pairs in a relation — for example, $R = \{(1,1),(1,2),(2,1),(2,2),(3,3)\}$ on the set $A = \{1,2,3\}$. But two relations can have the same elements and behave very differently in terms of structure. What makes one relation fair, reversible, or chainable? We need a precise vocabulary to describe those behaviors, but we don't have one yet. Can you tell, right now, whether the relation $R = \{(1,2),(2,3),(1,3)\}$ on $\{1,2,3\}$ is missing something important?
concept: A relation $R$ on a set $A$ can satisfy three key structural properties. Reflexivity means every element relates to itself. Symmetry means if $a$ relates to $b$, then $b$ relates back to $a$. Transitivity means if $a$ relates to $b$ and $b$ relates to $c$, then $a$ must relate to $c$. Formally: $R$ is reflexive if $\forall a \in A,\,(a,a)\in R$; symmetric if $(a,b)\in R \Rightarrow (b,a)\in R$; transitive if $(a,b)\in R$ and $(b,c)\in R \Rightarrow (a,c)\in R$.
anatomy: Each property has a precise structure worth naming. The reflexive property concerns the diagonal pairs — those of the form $(a,a)$, sometimes called self-loops; every element of the base set $A$ must appear as one. The symmetric property concerns the directionality of non-diagonal pairs: for every witness pair $(a,b)$ in $R$, its reverse $(b,a)$ must also be present. The transitive property concerns chains: a chain is a sequence of pairs $(a,b)$ and $(b,c)$ that share a middle element $b$; the chain forces a shortcut pair $(a,c)$ to exist in $R$.

worked_example.description_latex: We start by writing down the relation clearly so we can inspect it systematically. Having the set $A$ and all ordered pairs in front of us lets us check each property by asking targeted questions rather than guessing.
To check reflexivity, we list every element of $A$ and verify its diagonal pair is present. All three self-pairs appear, so the reflexive property holds — no element is left without a self-loop.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Symmetric? } (1,2)\in R \text{ and } (2,1)\in R;\quad \text{no other non-diagonal pairs} \implies \textbf{Yes}
worked_example.description_latex: For symmetry, we hunt for every non-diagonal pair and check that its reverse is also present. The only non-diagonal pair is $(1,2)$, and its mirror $(2,1)$ is indeed in $R$, so symmetry is satisfied.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Transitive? Check all chains: } (1,2)\in R,\,(2,1)\in R \Rightarrow (1,1)\in R\;\checkmark;\quad (2,1)\in R,\,(1,2)\in R \Rightarrow (2,2)\in R\;\checkmark
worked_example.description_latex: For transitivity, we try every possible chain $(a,b),(b,c)$ and verify $(a,c)$ exists. Every chain ends at a pair already present in $R$, so transitivity holds as well.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Conclusion: } R \text{ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive (an equivalence relation).}
worked_example.description_latex: When all three properties hold simultaneously, the relation is called an equivalence relation — a fact that will matter in later topics. For now, the key takeaway is the systematic three-step checking process we just applied.

partial_example.description_latex: We have a new relation with no diagonal pairs at all. Before checking anything, notice that $(1,1)$, $(2,2)$, and $(3,3)$ are all absent, which is already a hint about one of the three properties.
Since none of the three diagonal pairs appear in $R$, reflexivity fails immediately. We mark it not reflexive and move on to symmetry.

Now check symmetry: for each pair $(a,b)$ in $R$, determine whether $(b,a)$ is also in $R$. List every pair, write its reverse, and decide whether $R$ is symmetric or not symmetric. Show your reasoning.

Finally, check transitivity: identify every chain $(a,b),(b,c)$ in $R$ and verify whether the shortcut $(a,c)$ is present. State your conclusion — is $R$ transitive or not? Justify with a specific example or counterexample.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \{1,2\}$ and $R = \{(1,1),(2,2),(1,2),(2,1)\}$. Is $R$ reflexive, symmetric, and transitive?
practice_problems.answer_latex: Reflexive: yes (both diagonal pairs present). Symmetric: yes ($(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ both present). Transitive: yes (all chains close). $R$ is an equivalence relation.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \{1,2,3\}$ and $R = \{(1,1),(2,2),(3,3),(1,2),(2,3)\}$. Check each property and identify which ones hold.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Reflexive: yes. Symmetric: no, because $(1,2)\in R$ but $(2,1)\notin R$. Transitive: no, because $(1,2),(2,3)\in R$ but $(1,3)\notin R$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \{a,b,c\}$ and define $R$ by $(x,y)\in R$ if and only if $x = y$. Show that $R$ is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Reflexive: every element equals itself, so $(x,x)\in R$ for all $x$. Symmetric: if $x=y$ then $y=x$, so $(y,x)\in R$. Transitive: if $x=y$ and $y=z$ then $x=z$, so $(x,z)\in R$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: On $A = \mathbb{Z}$, define $R$ by $(a,b)\in R$ iff $a \leq b$. Determine which of the three properties hold and provide a counterexample for each that fails.
practice_problems.answer_latex: Reflexive: yes, $a \leq a$. Symmetric: no; counterexample $(1,2)\in R$ but $(2,1)\notin R$. Transitive: yes, $a\leq b$ and $b\leq c$ implies $a\leq c$.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A social network has users $\{P,Q,R\}$. Define 'follows' as: $P$ follows $Q$, $Q$ follows $R$, and $P$ follows $R$. No one follows themselves, and no reverse-follows exist. Represent this as a relation and determine which properties it satisfies.
practice_problems.answer_latex: The relation is $F = \{(P,Q),(Q,R),(P,R)\}$. Reflexive: no (no self-pairs). Symmetric: no (e.g., $P$ follows $Q$ but $Q$ does not follow $P$). Transitive: yes (the only chain is $(P,Q),(Q,R)$ and $(P,R)\in F$).

common_mistakes: Students forget to check reflexivity against every element of the base set $A$, not just the elements that appear in the relation. If $A = \{1,2,3\}$ but $3$ appears nowhere in $R$, reflexivity still fails because $(3,3)$ is missing — fix this by always listing the diagonal pairs for all elements of $A$ first.

Students test symmetry only on 'interesting' pairs and skip self-pairs like $(1,1)$, then incorrectly claim that $(1,1)$ breaks symmetry because there is no 'reverse' to check. In fact, $(a,a)$ is its own reverse and never violates symmetry — symmetry only fails when $(a,b)\in R$ with $a\neq b$ but $(b,a)\notin R$.

Students check only one chain when testing transitivity and conclude the property holds globally. For example, they verify $(1,2),(2,3)\Rightarrow(1,3)$ but forget to check whether $(2,1),(1,3)\Rightarrow(2,3)$ is also required — the fix is to systematically list every ordered pair as a potential middle element and check all chains that pass through it.
untested_variants: Checking properties of relations defined on infinite sets using set-builder or modular arithmetic notation, such as $(a,b)\in R$ iff $a \equiv b \pmod{n}$.
Using a matrix representation (Boolean adjacency matrix) to identify reflexive, symmetric, and transitive properties from the pattern of 1s on the diagonal or in symmetric positions.
Determining which properties are preserved or destroyed when two relations $R$ and $S$ are composed or unioned, e.g., deciding whether $R \cup S$ inherits transitivity.
Constructing the smallest relation that extends a given relation to make it satisfy one or more missing properties (i.e., computing reflexive, symmetric, or transitive closures).
